analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 8 finding(s) from 8 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: External debug requests can reach core CSR/register-file/control state through the debug unit without any observed authentication/authorization input; mediation is limited to whether the core is halted for protected internal accesses, while debug-visible control/settings are always accessible and halt/debug entry itself is driven by debug inputs.
findings.vulnerability_category: debug read/write of debug registers, GPRs, CSRs, and debug jump/redirect/halt-related control / core execution state, CSR state, register-file state, debug-visible control/status state / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: external debug/JTAG/test requester via zeroriscy_debug_unit debug interface Operation: debug read/write of debug registers, GPRs, CSRs, and debug jump/redirect/halt-related control Object: core execution state, CSR state, register-file state, debug-visible control/status state Expected guard: debug authentication/authorization or explicit debug-mode entry guard before debug requests can halt, inspect, or alter core state Observed behavior: zeroriscy_debug_unit exposes debug_req_i/debug_addr_i/debug_we_i/debug_wdata_i and grants many accesses; GPR/CSR/internal debug accesses are conditioned on debug_halted_o, but no authentication/authorization signal is visible. zeroriscy_core directly muxes debug CSR requests into csr_access/csr_addr/csr_wdata/csr_op and wires debug register write/jump requests into core control paths. Path: zeroriscy_debug_unit -> zeroriscy_core CSR mux/register-file/control interfaces Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An unauthenticated debug requester that can assert the debug interface can potentially halt the core, inspect/modify GPRs and CSRs, and redirect execution, bypassing normal software privilege boundaries.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full debug FSM entry/halt handling and top-level APB/JTAG debug access policy are not fully shown; no authentication signal was observed in available evidence.

findings.summary: CSR instructions appear to update machine CSRs by address decode and CSR operation selection without an observed current-privilege/access-permission check or read-only/write-restricted denial path; decoder only marks unsupported CSR op encodings as illegal and specially flags mstatus.
findings.vulnerability_category: CSR read/write/set/clear / mstatus, mepc, mcause, mtvec/mhartid read data, interrupt enable/trap state / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: software running on zero-riscy core via CSR instructions Operation: CSR read/write/set/clear Object: mstatus, mepc, mcause, mtvec/mhartid read data, interrupt enable/trap state Expected guard: machine/privilege-mode and CSR access permission checks, including read-only/write-restricted CSR enforcement Observed behavior: zeroriscy_decoder enables csr_access_o for CSR instructions and maps funct3 to CSR_OP_WRITE/SET/CLEAR; illegal only for default CSR op encoding. zeroriscy_cs_registers reads selected CSRs by address and writes mstatus/mepc/mcause when csr_we_int is set, with no observed privilege/current-mode check. Hardware exception updates have priority over software writes. Path: CSR instruction decode in zeroriscy_decoder -> zeroriscy_core CSR path -> zeroriscy_cs_registers write/read logic Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Software executing CSR instructions can alter interrupt enable and trap state (mstatus/mepc/mcause) if not otherwise constrained, potentially redirecting exception return or changing interrupt behavior without privilege mediation.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Implemented zero-riscy privilege model may be machine-only; full ID-stage handling of csr_status_o and illegal instruction response for CSR permissions was not fully evidenced.

findings.summary: Core load/store requests are routed through fc_demux by a one-bit port select and address-map constants exist, but no observed PMP/security/privilege mediation or error response is applied to loads/stores in the LSU/demux path.
findings.vulnerability_category: load/store read/write transaction routing / L2/TCDM/SCM/peripheral/outbound memory regions reachable from core data port / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: zero-riscy core data load/store requester Operation: load/store read/write transaction routing Object: L2/TCDM/SCM/peripheral/outbound memory regions reachable from core data port Expected guard: address-range decode plus security/privilege/PMP or wrapper mediation for protected memory/peripheral regions, consistently applied to loads and stores Observed behavior: zeroriscy_load_store_unit emits data_req_o, data_addr_o, data_we_o, data_be_o and ignores data_err_i for load/store error outputs. fc_subsystem wires core data bus into fc_demux_data_i. fc_demux routes requests to master_port0 or master_port1 based solely on port_sel_i, with grants/valids forwarded; no deny/error path is visible in shown demux logic. soc_bus_defines provides address ranges but no protection policy in observed path. Path: zeroriscy_load_store_unit data interface -> fc_subsystem core_data_bus -> fc_demux_data_i -> L2/SCM or fabric targets Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Core software may read or write any routed memory/MMIO region reachable through the data bus, including protected peripherals or memory windows, because denied accesses are not surfaced and no privilege/PMP check is observed.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full fc_demux selection logic after truncation and origin of is_scm_data_req/port_sel_i are not fully shown; upstream or downstream interconnect wrappers may enforce address permissions outside the observed scope.

findings.summary: Instruction fetch routing appears to use boot/PC selection and fc_demux instruction routing without an observed execute-permission, secure-region, or unmapped-address deny path.
findings.vulnerability_category: instruction fetch/execute routing / instruction memory/code regions and secure/protected executable regions reachable by core_instr_bus / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: zero-riscy instruction fetch unit Operation: instruction fetch/execute routing Object: instruction memory/code regions and secure/protected executable regions reachable by core_instr_bus Expected guard: execute/fetch permission or secure-mode address-range mediation for protected code regions, plus safe handling of unmapped/secure addresses Observed behavior: zeroriscy_if_stage selects fetch_addr_n from boot/exception/debug/jump PC sources and emits instr_req_o/instr_addr_o. fc_subsystem wires core_instr_addr/req/gnt/rvalid to fc_demux_instr_i, and fc_demux routes by port_sel_i without observed execute/security checks. Path: zeroriscy_if_stage instr_addr_o/instr_req_o -> fc_subsystem core_instr_bus -> fc_demux_instr_i -> instruction memory/fabric targets Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Software or debug-controlled PC redirection may fetch/execute from any address that the instruction demux routes, including protected or unmapped regions, if no external execute-permission check exists.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full IF-stage PC mux and fc_subsystem port-select address decode were truncated; no downstream memory-execute policy information was provided.

findings.summary: APB writes can update interrupt mask/int/ack state in apb_interrupt_cntrl and reads expose interrupt/FIFO state, while pready is always asserted and pslverr is always 0; no secure-mode/privilege guard on APB register access was observed.
findings.vulnerability_category: configure, trigger, acknowledge, and read interrupt controller state / interrupt mask/pending/ack registers, FIFO event register, core_irq_req_o/core_irq_id_o; fetch_en_o/core_clock_en_o are fixed enabled / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: APB/software-visible register requester and asynchronous event inputs Operation: configure, trigger, acknowledge, and read interrupt controller state Object: interrupt mask/pending/ack registers, FIFO event register, core_irq_req_o/core_irq_id_o; fetch_en_o/core_clock_en_o are fixed enabled Expected guard: secure-mode, privilege, or APB wrapper access mediation for configuring interrupt enables, security attribute, fetch enable, and core clock controls Observed behavior: apb_interrupt_cntrl decodes APB read/write accesses using psel/penable/pwrite and register address bits. It assigns core_clock_en_o=1 and fetch_en_o=1. APB read path exposes r_int/r_ack/r_mask/r_fifo_event. The APB slave always returns ready and never signals error. No use of core_secure_mode_i to gate APB register updates was observed in available snippets. Path: APB_BUS.Slave apb_slave -> apb_interrupt_cntrl register/control logic -> core_irq_req_o/core_irq_id_o/fetch_en_o/core_clock_en_o Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An APB requester with bus reachability can mask, set, clear, acknowledge, or observe interrupt state without local privilege/secure-mode denial, potentially suppressing or injecting interrupts and affecting control flow.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The middle write-update logic of apb_interrupt_cntrl was truncated in observations, so exact update equations and any hidden core_secure_mode_i use are not fully available; APB bus-level access control may exist outside this module.

findings.summary: components file defines BIST_BUS with bist_en, bist_pattern, done/result signals and defines cache control/bypass/flush interfaces. apb_interrupt_cntrl and fc_hwpe have test_mode_i/test_mode_i-like ports. However searches in fc_subsystem for 'bist' and 'bypass' returned no uses, so the implemented production path and guards were not evidenced in this scope.
findings.vulnerability_category: enable test/BIST, bypass/flush cache or memory path, observe test result / BIST bus signals, BRAM/cache flush/bypass control interfaces, potential memory/cache/TCDM contents / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: test interface or software/cache-control requester Operation: enable test/BIST, bypass/flush cache or memory path, observe test result Object: BIST bus signals, BRAM/cache flush/bypass control interfaces, potential memory/cache/TCDM contents Expected guard: test/lifecycle authorization or production-mode disable guard before BIST, bypass, flush, or direct memory test controls can affect protected memory or expose results Observed behavior: components file defines BIST_BUS with bist_en, bist_pattern, done/result signals and defines cache control/bypass/flush interfaces. apb_interrupt_cntrl and fc_hwpe have test_mode_i/test_mode_i-like ports. However searches in fc_subsystem for 'bist' and 'bypass' returned no uses, so the implemented production path and guards were not evidenced in this scope. Path: test_mode_i / BIST_BUS / cache bypass-flush interfaces (definitions only) -> connected memory/cache controls not observed Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If connected without lifecycle/test authorization, these interfaces could alter memory/cache behavior or expose BIST results, but the provided observations do not establish reachability in this scope.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: No connected BIST/bypass implementation or register path in fc_subsystem was observed; production-mode guard and result exposure cannot be determined from interface definitions alone.

findings.summary: The HWPE wrapper exposes accelerator TCDM master ports and APB configuration conversion to peripheral control, but no local address-range or requester-identity mediation is visible before HWPE memory requests are driven onto fabric master ports.
findings.vulnerability_category: accelerator/DMA-like memory read/write or control transaction / shared TCDM/L2/peripheral address space reachable by l2_hwpe_master/hwacc_xbar_master / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: HWPE/accelerator requester Operation: accelerator/DMA-like memory read/write or control transaction Object: shared TCDM/L2/peripheral address space reachable by l2_hwpe_master/hwacc_xbar_master Expected guard: requester identity/address-range mediation so HWPE transactions cannot access protected memory/peripheral regions beyond its allowed window Observed behavior: fc_hwpe has hwacc_xbar_master[N_MASTER_PORT] and APB configuration slave. It instantiates apb2per for configuration and mac_top_wrap, wiring tcdm_req/tcdm_add/tcdm_wen/tcdm_be/tcdm_wdata/tcdm_r_data between accelerator and master interface signals. No address policy, deny response, or range checks are visible in observed fc_hwpe snippet. fc_subsystem exposes l2_hwpe_master array. Path: APB hwacc_cfg_slave -> apb2per/mac_top_wrap -> tcdm_* -> hwacc_xbar_master/l2_hwpe_master fabric ports Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: A configured accelerator could issue reads/writes to shared memory windows beyond its intended region, potentially corrupting or disclosing protected memory, unless external fabric controls constrain it.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Remaining fc_hwpe lines and mac_top_wrap internals are truncated/not provided; external interconnect may impose ranges outside this module.

findings.summary: Secure interrupt classification appears inconsistent/stubbed: apb_interrupt_cntrl exposes core_secure_mode_i/core_irq_sec_o, but core_irq_sec_o has no observed assignment/use in the interrupt controller, and fc_subsystem ties the core's irq_sec_i input to 1'b0 rather than the controller's core_irq_sec signal.
findings.vulnerability_category: change/use security mode and interrupt security classification / core_irq_sec_o/core_irq_sec signal and core secure-mode/privilege state / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: interrupt/event source and software configuring interrupt state Operation: change/use security mode and interrupt security classification Object: core_irq_sec_o/core_irq_sec signal and core secure-mode/privilege state Expected guard: security attribution should be derived from trusted state and used consistently to restrict secure interrupt delivery/handling and non-secure influence Observed behavior: apb_interrupt_cntrl declares core_irq_sec_o and core_secure_mode_i, but search only found the core_irq_sec_o port declaration. fc_subsystem declares core_irq_sec but the zero-riscy core instance uses .irq_sec_i(1'b0) and leaves .sec_lvl_o unconnected. Thus secure IRQ attribution is not propagated to the core in observed wiring. Path: apb_interrupt_cntrl core_secure_mode_i/core_irq_sec_o -> fc_subsystem core_irq_sec -> zeroriscy_core irq_sec_i/sec_lvl_o Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Secure interrupt information may be ignored or forced non-secure, allowing incorrect interrupt handling policy or preventing secure-mode restrictions from being enforced consistently.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The second zero-riscy_core instance wiring after line 241 was truncated; full apb_interrupt_cntrl assignment region is partially truncated, but search observation found no core_irq_sec usage.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
